judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports hit all four expectations with comparable depth and precision; Report 1 adds a minor extra detail (infra/main.tf check) but the substance is equivalent.

report_1.evidence: Reminder messages will be sent automatically for it. The deciding code is auto_send_reminders_enabled in slotwise/backend/app/feature_flags.py, lines 10–13: line 11 checks tenant.settings.get("auto_send_reminders") is None, and line 12 — return True — is the exact line that decides it.

report_2.evidence: Reminders will be sent automatically. The deciding code is auto_send_reminders_enabled in backend/app/feature_flags.py, specifically lines 11–12: if tenant.settings.get("auto_send_reminders") is None: return True

report_1.evidence: The change I would make is in backend/app/feature_flags.py lines 11–12: replace the is None → return True branch so a missing value resolves to False (the whole function can collapse to return bool(tenant.settings.get("auto_send_reminders")), which also makes malformed values fail safe).

report_2.evidence: I would change backend/app/feature_flags.py:11-12 so the None case returns False (or, more simply, return bool(tenant.settings.get("auto_send_reminders"))).

report_1.evidence: Commit 25509a7 ("SLOT-190 tenant settings and calendar view preference", 2026-06-10) created feature_flags.py with one function, compact_calendar_view_enabled, whose is None → return True default is reasonable for a cosmetic calendar-view preference... Inheriting a UI preference's "grandfather existing tenants in as on" semantics into a flag that fires outbound SMS/email is exactly the failure mode where the null state does the opposite of what the toggle's unset appearance implies.

report_2.evidence: Commit aa4cf8d ... then added auto_send_reminders_enabled as a line-for-line clone of that function... Default-on is defensible for compact_calendar_view — a reversible, tenant-internal display preference where the product wants the new view by default. Copying that default onto a flag that gates outbound patient messaging imports the wrong risk profile: the cost of a wrong "on" is unsolicited messages to real patients, not a layout quirk.

report_1.evidence: Add an environment-level master kill switch ANDed with the per-tenant value. Today reminders.py has no gate other than the tenant setting, and infra/main.tf defines nothing for this feature — there is no single switch that turns reminders off globally if sends misbehave.

report_2.evidence: Add a global kill switch. The batch job in reminders.py has no environment-level gate — the only control is per-tenant. Before a real rollout you want a way to stop all sends immediately without editing every tenant's settings.
